import { NextRequest } from 'next/server';
import { Prisma } from '@generated/prisma/client';
import { prisma } from '@backend/prisma';
import { SubscriptionStatus, BillingType } from '@generated/prisma/client';
import { getMessage } from '@backend/i18n';
import logger from '@root/lib/logger/logger.server';
import { validateCronRequestOrRespond } from '@root/lib/middleware/cronAuth';
import { NotificationTypes } from '@backend/notifications/types';
import { NotificationService } from '@backend/notifications/NotificationService';
import { getRenewalFailedExpiryDays, getTrialModeExtensionDays } from '@backend/settings';
import { assertSubscriptionDateRange } from '@backend/subscriptions';
import { monetizationPolicyService } from '@backend/monetization/MonetizationPolicyService';
import { ensureMonetizationPolicy, getEffectiveMonetizationPolicy } from '@backend/monetization/MonetizationPolicyResolver';
import { RECURRING_GRACE_HOURS } from '@backend/monetization/constants';
import { jsonResponse, errorResponse } from '@root/lib/api/responseHelpers';
import { emitSubscriptionStatusChanged } from '@/lib/pusher-server';
import { AnalyticsService } from '@/lib/analytics/service';
import { AnalyticsEvent } from '@/lib/analytics/types';
import { CronErrorCode } from '../errorCodes';

const analyticsService = new AnalyticsService();

const BATCH_SIZE = 50;
const CONCURRENCY_LIMIT = 10;

type BatchResult = { success: boolean; subscriptionId: string; skipped?: boolean };

/** Process items with a concurrency limit, returning settled results */
async function withConcurrencyLimit<T>(
  items: T[],
  limit: number,
  fn: (item: T) => Promise<BatchResult>,
): Promise<PromiseSettledResult<BatchResult>[]> {
  const results: PromiseSettledResult<BatchResult>[] = [];
  const executing: Promise<void>[] = [];

  for (const item of items) {
    const p = fn(item)
      .then((value) => { results.push({ status: 'fulfilled', value }); })
      .catch((reason) => { results.push({ status: 'rejected', reason }); })
      .then(() => { executing.splice(executing.indexOf(p), 1); });
    executing.push(p);
    if (executing.length >= limit) await Promise.race(executing);
  }
  await Promise.all(executing);
  return results;
}

/** Fetch subscriptions in cursor-based batches and process each item */
async function processBatched<T extends { id: string }>(
  where: Prisma.SubscriptionWhereInput,
  include: Prisma.SubscriptionInclude,
  processFn: (subscription: T) => Promise<BatchResult>,
): Promise<PromiseSettledResult<BatchResult>[]> {
  const allResults: PromiseSettledResult<BatchResult>[] = [];
  let cursor: string | undefined;

  // eslint-disable-next-line no-constant-condition
  while (true) {
    const batch = await prisma.subscription.findMany({
      where,
      include,
      take: BATCH_SIZE,
      ...(cursor ? { skip: 1, cursor: { id: cursor } } : {}),
      orderBy: { id: 'asc' },
    }) as unknown as T[];

    if (batch.length === 0) break;

    const batchResults = await withConcurrencyLimit(batch, CONCURRENCY_LIMIT, processFn);
    allResults.push(...batchResults);

    if (batch.length < BATCH_SIZE) break;
    cursor = batch[batch.length - 1].id;
  }

  return allResults;
}

type SubscriptionWithUserAndPlan = Prisma.SubscriptionGetPayload<{
  include: { user: true; plan: true };
}>;

// This endpoint is designed to be called by a cron job service like Vercel Cron Jobs
// It will check for subscriptions that have expired but still marked as active
export async function GET(request: NextRequest) {
  try {
    // Validate cron authentication
    const authError = validateCronRequestOrRespond(request, 'expire-subscriptions');
    if (authError) {
      return authError;
    }

    // Section 1: Detect suspected renewal failures
    const RECURRING_GRACE_MS = RECURRING_GRACE_HOURS * 60 * 60 * 1000;
    const graceThreshold = new Date(Date.now() - RECURRING_GRACE_MS);

    const renewalFailureResults = await processBatched<SubscriptionWithUserAndPlan>(
      {
        billingType: BillingType.RECURRING,
        status: SubscriptionStatus.ACTIVE,
        endDate: { lt: graceThreshold },
      },
      { user: true, plan: true },
      async (subscription) => {
        try {
          const { count } = await prisma.subscription.updateMany({
            where: { id: subscription.id, status: SubscriptionStatus.ACTIVE },
            data: {
              status: SubscriptionStatus.RENEWAL_FAILED,
              renewalFailedAt: new Date(),
            },
          });

          if (count === 0) return { success: true, subscriptionId: subscription.id, skipped: true };

          await monetizationPolicyService.invalidateAllCaches(subscription.userId);
          emitSubscriptionStatusChanged(subscription.userId, { status: 'RENEWAL_FAILED', accessLevel: 'RENEWAL_FAILED' });

          // Send RENEWAL_FAILED notification
          if (subscription.user.telegramId) {
            const notificationService = new NotificationService({ prisma });
            await notificationService.send(subscription.user, NotificationTypes.RENEWAL_FAILED_NOTIFICATION, {
              subscriptionId: subscription.id,
            });
          }

          // Analytics
          analyticsService.track(AnalyticsEvent.SUBSCRIPTION_RENEWAL_FAILED, {
            subscription_id: subscription.id,
            plan_id: subscription.planId,
            detected_by: 'cron' as const,
          }, subscription.userId).catch(() => {});

          return { success: true, subscriptionId: subscription.id };
        } catch (error) {
          logger.error(`Error marking renewal failure for subscription ${subscription.id}:`, {
            error: error instanceof Error ? error.message : String(error),
          });
          return { success: false, subscriptionId: subscription.id };
        }
      },
    );

    const renewalFailureSuccessCount = renewalFailureResults.filter(
      (result) => result.status === 'fulfilled' && result.value.success,
    ).length;

    logger.info(`Processed ${renewalFailureResults.length} suspected renewal failures (${renewalFailureSuccessCount} successful)`);

    // Section 2: Transition RENEWAL_FAILED → EXPIRED after grace period
    const renewalFailedExpiryDays = await getRenewalFailedExpiryDays();
    const renewalFailedThreshold = new Date(Date.now() - renewalFailedExpiryDays * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000);

    const renewalExpiredResults = await processBatched<SubscriptionWithUserAndPlan>(
      {
        status: SubscriptionStatus.RENEWAL_FAILED,
        renewalFailedAt: { lt: renewalFailedThreshold },
      },
      { user: true, plan: true },
      async (subscription) => {
        try {
          // Atomic: status flip + policy snapshot in same transaction
          const { count } = await prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => {
            const result = await tx.subscription.updateMany({
              where: { id: subscription.id, status: SubscriptionStatus.RENEWAL_FAILED },
              data: { status: SubscriptionStatus.EXPIRED },
            });
            if (result.count > 0) {
              await ensureMonetizationPolicy(subscription.userId, subscription.user.registeredBot ?? 'am', tx as typeof prisma);
            }
            return result;
          });

          if (count === 0) return { success: true, subscriptionId: subscription.id, skipped: true };

          await monetizationPolicyService.invalidateAllCaches(subscription.userId);
          emitSubscriptionStatusChanged(subscription.userId, { status: 'EXPIRED', accessLevel: 'LIMITED' });

          analyticsService.track(AnalyticsEvent.SUBSCRIPTION_EXPIRED, {
            subscription_id: subscription.id,
            plan_id: subscription.planId,
            billing_type: subscription.billingType as 'RECURRING' | 'ONE_TIME',
            plan_type: subscription.plan.planType,
            is_trial: subscription.plan.planType === 'TRIAL',
          }, subscription.userId).catch(() => {});

          return { success: true, subscriptionId: subscription.id };
        } catch (error) {
          logger.error(`Error expiring renewal-failed subscription ${subscription.id}:`, {
            error: error instanceof Error ? error.message : String(error),
          });
          return { success: false, subscriptionId: subscription.id };
        }
      },
    );

    const renewalExpiredSuccessCount = renewalExpiredResults.filter(
      (result) => result.status === 'fulfilled' && result.value.success,
    ).length;

    logger.info(`Processed ${renewalExpiredResults.length} RENEWAL_FAILED→EXPIRED transitions (${renewalExpiredSuccessCount} successful)`);

    // Section 3: Expire active/canceled subscriptions past endDate
    const trialModeExtensionDays = await getTrialModeExtensionDays();
    if (trialModeExtensionDays > 0) {
      logger.info(`Trial mode enabled: extending trial subscriptions by ${trialModeExtensionDays} days`);
    }

    const results = await processBatched<SubscriptionWithUserAndPlan>(
      {
        OR: [
          {
            status: SubscriptionStatus.ACTIVE,
            endDate: { lt: new Date() },
            NOT: { billingType: BillingType.RECURRING },
          },
          {
            status: SubscriptionStatus.CANCELED,
            endDate: { lt: new Date() },
          },
        ],
      },
      { user: true, plan: true },
      async (subscription) => {
        try {
          // Trial mode: extend trial subscriptions instead of expiring them
          if (trialModeExtensionDays > 0 && subscription.plan.planType === 'TRIAL') {
            const newEndDate = new Date(subscription.endDate);
            newEndDate.setDate(newEndDate.getDate() + trialModeExtensionDays);
            assertSubscriptionDateRange(subscription.startDate, newEndDate);

            await prisma.subscription.update({
              where: { id: subscription.id },
              data: { endDate: newEndDate },
            });

            await monetizationPolicyService.invalidateAllCaches(subscription.userId);

            logger.info(`Extended trial subscription ${subscription.id} by ${trialModeExtensionDays} days`, {
              oldEndDate: subscription.endDate.toISOString(),
              newEndDate: newEndDate.toISOString(),
            });

            return { success: true, subscriptionId: subscription.id };
          }

          // Atomic: status flip + policy snapshot in same transaction
          const { count } = await prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => {
            const result = await tx.subscription.updateMany({
              where: {
                id: subscription.id,
                status: { in: [SubscriptionStatus.ACTIVE, SubscriptionStatus.CANCELED] },
              },
              data: { status: SubscriptionStatus.EXPIRED },
            });
            if (result.count > 0) {
              await ensureMonetizationPolicy(subscription.userId, subscription.user.registeredBot ?? 'am', tx as typeof prisma);
            }
            return result;
          });

          if (count === 0) return { success: true, subscriptionId: subscription.id, skipped: true };

          await monetizationPolicyService.invalidateAllCaches(subscription.userId);
          emitSubscriptionStatusChanged(subscription.userId, { status: 'EXPIRED', accessLevel: 'LIMITED' });

          // Send expiration notification with dynamic policy values
          if (subscription.user.telegramId) {
            const notificationService = new NotificationService({ prisma });
            const notificationType = subscription.plan.planType === 'TRIAL'
              ? NotificationTypes.TRIAL_ENDED_NOTIFICATION
              : NotificationTypes.SUBSCRIPTION_ENDED_NOTIFICATION;
            const policy = await getEffectiveMonetizationPolicy(subscription.userId, subscription.user.registeredBot ?? undefined);
            await notificationService.send(subscription.user, notificationType, {
              subscriptionId: subscription.id,
              swapLimit: String(policy.monthlySwapLimit),
              requiredFriends: String(policy.requiredFriends),
              requiredBooks: String(policy.requiredBooks),
              rewardDuration: String(policy.rewardDurationDays),
            });
          }

          // Analytics
          analyticsService.track(AnalyticsEvent.SUBSCRIPTION_EXPIRED, {
            subscription_id: subscription.id,
            plan_id: subscription.planId,
            billing_type: subscription.billingType as 'RECURRING' | 'ONE_TIME',
            plan_type: subscription.plan.planType,
            is_trial: subscription.plan.planType === 'TRIAL',
          }, subscription.userId).catch(() => {});

          return { success: true, subscriptionId: subscription.id };
        } catch (error) {
          logger.error(`Error processing subscription ${subscription.id}:`, {
            error: error instanceof Error ? error.message : String(error),
          });

          // Always invalidate cache even on error
          await monetizationPolicyService.invalidateAllCaches(subscription.userId);

          return { success: false, subscriptionId: subscription.id };
        }
      },
    );

    const successCount = results.filter((result) => result.status === 'fulfilled' && result.value.success).length;

    logger.info(`Processed ${results.length} expired subscriptions (${successCount} successful)`);

    return jsonResponse(request, {
      success: true,
      renewalFailures: {
        processed: renewalFailureResults.length,
        successful: renewalFailureSuccessCount,
        failed: renewalFailureResults.length - renewalFailureSuccessCount,
      },
      renewalExpired: {
        processed: renewalExpiredResults.length,
        successful: renewalExpiredSuccessCount,
        failed: renewalExpiredResults.length - renewalExpiredSuccessCount,
      },
      subscriptions: {
        processed: results.length,
        successful: successCount,
        failed: results.length - successCount,
      },
    });
  } catch (error) {
    logger.error('Error expiring subscriptions:', { error: error instanceof Error ? error.message : String(error) });
    return errorResponse(
      request,
      {
        errorCode: CronErrorCode.SUBSCRIPTION_EXPIRATION_FAILED,
        message: getMessage({ locale: 'en', path: 'subscription.errors.expirationProcessFailed' }),
      },
      500,
    );
  }
}

web search: site:developer.apple.com App Store Server Notifications V2 signedPayload documentation

codex
**Recommendation**

Build a provider-neutral subscription domain and move Telegram Stars behind the same interface as Apple and Google. Do not add Apple/Google as special cases inside the existing Stars handlers. The current system already has useful pieces: `Subscription`, `SubscriptionPayment`, plan records, idempotency, cache invalidation, Pusher subscription updates, and expiration cron. Keep those, but separate “entitlement state” from “provider transaction state.”

The core rule should be:

> Chapta grants access from its own normalized subscription entitlement table, but that table is updated only from verified provider facts.

Client purchase callbacks and webhooks are inputs. They are not the source of truth.

**Why This Shape**

Apple and Google both have asynchronous, eventually consistent lifecycle events. Apple sends signed App Store Server Notifications V2 payloads and exposes signed transaction/subscription data through the App Store Server API. Apple’s older `verifyReceipt` flow is deprecated in favor of App Store Server API / signed StoreKit transactions. Google recommends server-side purchase verification, storing every purchase token, and using `purchases.subscriptionsv2.get`; Real-time Developer Notifications are signals that must be followed by a Developer API lookup.

Official references:

- Apple App Store Server Notifications V2 use `signedPayload` JWS: https://developer.apple.com/documentation/appstoreservernotifications/receiving-app-store-server-notifications
- Apple App Store Server API returns signed transaction and renewal info: https://developer.apple.com/documentation/appstoreserverapi
- Apple receipt validation / `verifyReceipt` is deprecated: https://developer.apple.com/documentation/AppStoreReceipts
- Apple StoreKit `Transaction.jwsRepresentation` can be sent to your server for verification: https://developer.apple.com/documentation/storekit/transaction
- Google `purchases.subscriptionsv2.get`: https://developers.google.com/android-publisher/api-ref/rest/v3/purchases.subscriptionsv2/get
- Google purchase verification and acknowledgement guidance: https://developer.android.com/google/play/billing/security
- Google RTDN must be followed by Developer API lookup: https://developer.android.com/google/play/billing/rtdn-reference

**Target Architecture**

```text
Native iOS App
  StoreKit 2 purchase
  sends signed transaction JWS
        |
        v
POST /api/subscriptions/providers/apple/verify
        |
        v
AppleProviderAdapter.verifyClientPurchase()
        |
        v
SubscriptionApplicationService.applyProviderEvent()
        |
        v
Normalized DB entitlement + payment/event tables
        |
        v
Invalidate Redis + emit Pusher + analytics

Apple Server Notification V2
        |
        v
POST /api/webhooks/subscriptions/apple
        |
        v
Verify Apple signedPayload JWS
Fetch latest status if needed
        |
        v
Same SubscriptionApplicationService.applyProviderEvent()
```

```text
Native Android App
  Play Billing purchase
  sends purchaseToken + productId
        |
        v
POST /api/subscriptions/providers/google/verify
        |
        v
GoogleProviderAdapter.verifyClientPurchase()
  calls purchases.subscriptionsv2.get
  acknowledges if needed
        |
        v
SubscriptionApplicationService.applyProviderEvent()
        |
        v
Normalized DB entitlement + payment/event tables

Google Pub/Sub RTDN
        |
        v
POST /api/webhooks/subscriptions/google
        |
        v
Verify Pub/Sub push identity/signature
Decode message
Call purchases.subscriptionsv2.get
        |
        v
Same SubscriptionApplicationService.applyProviderEvent()
```

```text
Telegram Mini App
  create Stars invoice
  Telegram successful_payment update
        |
        v
TelegramProviderAdapter.processPayment()
        |
        v
Same SubscriptionApplicationService.applyProviderEvent()
```

The critical design point: all providers enter through provider-specific adapters, but all state mutation goes through one application service.

**Domain Boundaries**

Use these layers:

```text
src/lib/backend/subscriptions/
  domain/
    SubscriptionEntitlement.ts
    ProviderSubscriptionEvent.ts
    SubscriptionStateMachine.ts
  application/
    SubscriptionApplicationService.ts
    ApplyProviderEvent.ts
    VerifyClientPurchase.ts
    CancelSubscription.ts
  providers/
    telegram/
      TelegramStarsAdapter.ts
    apple/
      AppleIapAdapter.ts
      AppleNotificationVerifier.ts
      AppleServerApiClient.ts
    google/
      GooglePlayAdapter.ts
      GoogleRtdnVerifier.ts
      GooglePlayApiClient.ts
  persistence/
    SubscriptionRepository.ts
    ProviderEventRepository.ts
```

Routes should become thin orchestrators:

```ts
export async function POST(request: NextRequest) {
  const user = await authenticateRequest(request);
  const body = await parseAndValidate(request, appleVerifySchema);

  const result = await subscriptionPurchaseService.verifyClientPurchase({
    provider: 'APPLE_APP_STORE',
    userId: user.id,
    payload: body,
  });

  return jsonResponse(request, result, 200);
}
```

Do not let route handlers directly update `subscription`, `subscription_payment`, Redis, Pusher, or analytics. That is the job of the application service.

**Provider Model**

Add an explicit provider dimension. Today the schema has `telegramChargeId`, `providerChargeId`, and `billingType`, which will not scale cleanly.

Recommended normalized concepts:

```ts
type SubscriptionProvider =
  | 'TELEGRAM_STARS'
  | 'APPLE_APP_STORE'
  | 'GOOGLE_PLAY';

type ProviderEnvironment =
  | 'PRODUCTION'
  | 'SANDBOX';

type ProviderEventType =
  | 'PURCHASED'
  | 'RENEWED'
  | 'CANCELED'
  | 'EXPIRED'
  | 'REFUNDED'
  | 'REVOKED'
  | 'GRACE_PERIOD'
  | 'ACCOUNT_HOLD'
  | 'RECOVERED'
  | 'PAUSED'
  | 'PRICE_CHANGE'
  | 'UNKNOWN';
```

At minimum, the DB needs either new fields or new tables for:

```text
Subscription
  provider
  providerSubscriptionId
  providerOriginalTransactionId
  providerPurchaseToken
  providerProductId
  providerEnvironment
  autoRenewEnabled
  canceledAt
  revokedAt
  latestProviderEventAt
  sourcePlatform

SubscriptionPayment / SubscriptionTransaction
  provider
  providerTransactionId
  providerOriginalTransactionId
  providerOrderId
  providerPurchaseToken
  providerProductId
  amountMicros
  currency
  purchasedAt
  expiresAt
  rawEventId / rawPayloadRef

SubscriptionProviderEvent
  provider
  environment
  eventId
  eventType
  providerSubscriptionKey
  providerTransactionId
  occurredAt
  receivedAt
  processedAt
  processingStatus
  rawPayload
  normalizedPayload
  unique(provider, environment, eventId)
```

I would avoid overloading `telegramChargeId` for all providers. Keep it temporarily for compatibility if needed, but introduce provider-neutral fields and migrate code toward them.

**Plan Mapping**

Do not assume one Chapta plan ID equals one platform product ID.

Use a mapping table:

```text
SubscriptionPlan
  id
  name
  durationDays
  billingType
  planType
  isActive

SubscriptionPlanProviderProduct
  id
  planId
  provider
  environment
  productId
  basePlanId       // Google
  offerId          // Google, optional
  subscriptionGroupId // Apple, optional
  isActive
  metadata
  unique(provider, environment, productId, basePlanId, offerId)
```

Reason: Apple products, Google products/base plans/offers, and Telegram Stars plan IDs have different identity systems. This also lets you hide Apple plans from Telegram users and vice versa without `botId` becoming the only selector.

**State Machine**

Your current statuses are:

```text
ACTIVE
CANCELED
EXPIRED
RENEWAL_FAILED
```

That is enough for the public entitlement surface, but too coarse for provider lifecycle. Add either more internal statuses or separate provider state.

Recommended public entitlement states:

```text
ACTIVE          grants full access
CANCELED        grants access until endDate, no auto-renew
GRACE_PERIOD    grants access temporarily, payment retry in progress
ON_HOLD         limited/no access, provider says payment problem
EXPIRED         no paid access
REVOKED         no access, refund/reversal/fraud
```

If you want to avoid changing public API initially, you can map internally:

```text
Provider state                 Chapta entitlement
Apple DID_RENEW                ACTIVE
Apple DID_FAIL_TO_RENEW grace  GRACE_PERIOD or ACTIVE with grace flag
Apple EXPIRED                  EXPIRED
Apple REFUND / REVOKE          REVOKED
Google ACTIVE                  ACTIVE
Google IN_GRACE_PERIOD         GRACE_PERIOD
Google ON_HOLD                 ON_HOLD / RENEWAL_FAILED
Google CANCELED with expiry    CANCELED
Google EXPIRED                 EXPIRED
Telegram recurring renewal     ACTIVE
Telegram renewal missed        RENEWAL_FAILED
```

The state machine should reject stale provider events. Provider events arrive out of order. Store `occurredAt` / provider signed date and ignore state regressions unless the event is explicitly revocation/refund.

**Receipt / Purchase Validation**

For iOS:

1. App uses StoreKit 2.
2. After purchase, send `transaction.jwsRepresentation` to backend.
3. Backend verifies Apple JWS signature using Apple’s server library or equivalent.
4. Backend checks bundle ID, environment, product ID, transaction ID, original transaction ID, purchase date, expiration date, revocation date.
5. Backend optionally calls App Store Server API `Get Transaction Info`, `Get Transaction History`, or `Get All Subscription Statuses` to reconcile current state.
6. Backend maps product ID to Chapta plan.
7. Backend applies normalized event.

Do not build new code around `verifyReceipt`; Apple marks that path deprecated.

For Android:

1. App uses Play Billing.
2. Send `purchaseToken`, package name, product ID/base plan/offer details to backend.
3. Backend calls `purchases.subscriptionsv2.get`.
4. Backend verifies package name, product ID, expiry, subscription state, acknowledgement state, linked purchase token, obfuscated account ID if used.
5. Backend acknowledges the subscription from the backend when required.
6. Backend stores the purchase token as unique.
7. If `linkedPurchaseToken` exists, revoke/deactivate entitlement tied to the old token, per Google guidance.
8. Backend applies normalized event.

For Telegram:

The existing Telegram successful payment handler already performs useful checks: idempotency by charge ID, plan lookup, bot ownership, price drift, billing type drift, cache invalidation. Keep those behaviors, but move them into a `TelegramStarsAdapter` that emits normalized events.

**Webhook Handling**

Use provider-specific webhook endpoints, but one common processing pipeline.

```text
/api/webhooks/subscriptions/apple
/api/webhooks/subscriptions/google
/api/webhooks/subscriptions/telegram   // if you separate bot updates later
```

Webhook rules:

1. Verify authenticity before parsing business meaning.
2. Insert raw event first with unique idempotency key.
3. If duplicate, return success immediately.
4. Normalize provider payload into `ProviderSubscriptionEvent`.
5. Fetch latest provider state when the webhook is only a signal.
6. Apply event in a DB transaction.
7. Post-commit: invalidate Redis, emit Pusher, analytics, notifications.
8. Never let post-commit side effects cause provider retry if the DB commit succeeded.

Google RTDN specifically should not directly mutate subscription state from the Pub/Sub message alone. Treat it as a nudge to call Google Play Developer API for authoritative status.

Apple notifications include signed data, but I would still use Server API reconciliation for ambiguous lifecycle changes, missed notifications, or account recovery flows.

**Idempotency Keys**

Use provider-specific keys:

```text
Telegram:
  provider = TELEGRAM_STARS
  key = telegramChargeId

Apple:
  provider = APPLE_APP_STORE
  key = notificationUUID for notifications
  transaction key = originalTransactionId + transactionId + environment

Google:
  provider = GOOGLE_PLAY
  key = purchaseToken for purchase identity
  event key = Pub/Sub messageId or packageName + eventTimeMillis + purchaseToken + notificationType
```

Do not use user ID + plan ID as idempotency for provider purchases. A user can renew the same product many times.

**Subscription Ownership**

You need a strong account-linking story.

Apple:
- Use `appAccountToken` where possible so transactions can be associated with the Chapta user.
- Still support restore purchases, because Apple subscriptions are owned by Apple ID and may appear on a new device.

Google:
- Set `obfuscatedAccountId` / `obfuscatedProfileId` in BillingFlowParams.
- Verify it on the backend when `externalAccountIdentifiers` are present.
- Store every `purchaseToken`; Google explicitly calls tokens globally unique and recommends keeping records.

Telegram:
- Current `userId` in invoice payload plus Telegram bot context is acceptable, but continue validating bot/plan affinity.

Policy decision needed: can the same paid provider subscription be transferred between Chapta users? I recommend no automatic transfer. If a restore finds a provider subscription already linked to another Chapta account, return a support-required conflict.

**Entitlement Resolution**

The app should call one endpoint:

```text
GET /api/subscriptions/status
```

That endpoint should not care whether the entitlement came from Telegram, Apple, Google, referral, or trial.

Response can include provider metadata for UI:

```ts
type SubscriptionStatusResponse = {
  status: 'ACTIVE' | 'CANCELED' | 'GRACE_PERIOD' | 'ON_HOLD' | 'EXPIRED' | 'REVOKED' | 'NONE';
  accessLevel: 'FULL' | 'LIMITED' | 'NONE';
  provider: 'TELEGRAM_STARS' | 'APPLE_APP_STORE' | 'GOOGLE_PLAY' | 'TRIAL' | 'REFERRAL' | null;
  planId: string | null;
  productId: string | null;
  endDate: string | null;
  autoRenewEnabled: boolean | null;
  managementUrl: string | null;
};
```

Cancellation UI should be provider-aware:

```text
Telegram recurring:
  in-app cancel can call Telegram API if supported

Apple:
  show App Store subscription management flow/link
  local backend should not pretend cancellation succeeded until Apple event/API confirms it

Google:
  show Play subscription management flow/link
  backend developer cancellation may be possible, but user-initiated cancellation should usually go through Play
```

The current cancellation handler directly marks `ACTIVE -> CANCELED` before provider cancellation. That is acceptable for Telegram if local cancellation is the command source, but it is risky as a general pattern. For Apple/Google, cancellation state should come from provider confirmation or explicit provider API success.

**Reconciliation Jobs**

Keep the expiration cron, but reduce its authority for provider subscriptions.

Recommended scheduled jobs:

```text
Hourly:
  Find active provider subscriptions ending soon/past due.
  Re-query Apple/Google current status.
  Apply normalized state.

Daily:
  Reconcile subscriptions with stale latestProviderEventAt.
  Backfill missed webhooks.
  Detect DB/provider drift.

Weekly:
  Audit provider transactions without matching payments.
  Audit active entitlement with expired provider state.
```

The existing cron that marks recurring subscriptions `RENEWAL_FAILED` after endDate + grace works for Telegram Stars where your system may infer missing renewal. For Apple/Google, prefer provider state from APIs. Use cron as a reconciliation safety net, not the primary lifecycle driver.

**Migration Strategy**

1. Add provider-neutral schema alongside current Telegram fields.
2. Add `SubscriptionApplicationService.applyProviderEvent()` and route Telegram payment processing through it.
3. Add provider event table and idempotent event processing.
4. Add plan-provider product mapping.
5. Add Apple client-purchase verification endpoint.
6. Add Apple Server Notifications V2 endpoint.
7. Add Google client-purchase verification endpoint.
8. Add Google RTDN endpoint.
9. Update status/cancellation UI to branch by provider.
10. Add reconciliation jobs.
11. After all providers use the normalized path, deprecate direct reads of `telegramChargeId` except migration/backcompat.

**Code Shape Suggestion**

```ts
export interface SubscriptionProviderAdapter<TInput, TVerified> {
  provider: SubscriptionProvider;

  verifyClientPurchase(input: TInput): Promise<TVerified>;

  normalizeVerifiedPurchase(
    purchase: TVerified,
  ): ProviderSubscriptionEvent;

  fetchCurrentState(
    providerSubscriptionKey: string,
  ): Promise<ProviderSubscriptionSnapshot>;

  cancel?(
    providerSubscriptionKey: string,
  ): Promise<ProviderCancellationResult>;
}
```

```ts
export async function applyProviderEvent(
  event: ProviderSubscriptionEvent,
): Promise<ApplyProviderEventResult> {
  return prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => {
    const inserted = await providerEventRepository.insertIfNew(tx, event);
    if (!inserted) return { status: 'duplicate' };

    const planMapping = await planMappingRepository.findByProviderProduct(tx, {
      provider: event.provider,
      environment: event.environment,
      productId: event.productId,
      basePlanId: event.basePlanId,
      offerId: event.offerId,
    });

    const nextState = subscriptionStateMachine.reduce({
      current: await subscriptionRepository.findCurrentForProviderKey(tx, event.providerKey),
      event,
      planMapping,
    });

    await subscriptionRepository.save(tx, nextState.subscription);
    await paymentRepository.insertTransactions(tx, nextState.transactions);

    return {
      status: 'applied',
      userId: nextState.subscription.userId,
      publicStatus: nextState.publicStatus,
    };
  });
}
```

Post-commit wrapper:

```ts
const result = await applyProviderEvent(event);

if (result.status === 'applied') {
  await monetizationPolicyService.invalidateAllCaches(result.userId);
  await redis.operations.invalidateAllUserData(result.userId);
  emitSubscriptionStatusChanged(result.userId, {
    status: result.publicStatus,
    accessLevel: result.accessLevel,
  });
}
```

**Main Risks**

The largest risk is double entitlement. Google upgrades/resubscribes can produce linked purchase tokens; Apple restores can surface existing transactions on another device; Telegram subscriptions are tied to bot/user context. The system must enforce one active provider subscription identity maps to one Chapta user.

Second risk is stale local state. Webhooks can be delayed, dropped, duplicated, or out of order. This is why raw event storage, provider API reconciliation, and stale-event guards matter.

Third risk is provider-specific cancellation semantics. A local “cancel” button cannot mean the same thing for Telegram, Apple, and Google. Model cancellation as a provider command plus eventual provider-confirmed state.

**Bottom Line**

Architect this as a multi-provider entitlement system, not as “Telegram Stars plus two more payment handlers.” The DB should store normalized subscriptions, payments, provider events, provider identity keys, and plan-product mappings. Apple/Google/Telegram should be adapters that verify provider facts and emit normalized events. One application service should own subscription state transitions, cache invalidation, Pusher updates, analytics, and idempotency.

opencode.content:
**Current Constraints (from code)**
1. Payments are Telegram-centric today: idempotency keys and persistence depend on `telegramChargeId` ([schema.prisma](/Users/rsboarder/workspace/bookmatcher/packages/db/prisma/schema.prisma), [subscription-payment-handler.ts](/Users/rsboarder/workspace/bookmatcher/apps/tma/src/lib/telegram/bot/subscription-payment-handler.ts)).
2. Cancellation path directly calls Telegram Bot API for recurring subs ([cancelSubscriptionHandler.ts](/Users/rsboarder/workspace/bookmatcher/apps/tma/src/app/api/subscriptions/cancel/handlers/cancelSubscriptionHandler.ts)).
3. Subscription lifecycle (`ACTIVE/CANCELED/RENEWAL_FAILED/EXPIRED`) is already centralized enough to extend ([route.ts](/Users/rsboarder/workspace/bookmatcher/apps/tma/src/app/api/cron/expire-subscriptions/route.ts)).

**Recommended Multi-Provider Architecture**
1. Add a provider-agnostic core model:
- `SubscriptionSource` table: `provider` (`TELEGRAM_STARS|APPLE_IAP|GOOGLE_PLAY`), `externalSubscriptionId`, `originalTransactionId/purchaseToken`, `userId`, `planId`, `autoRenew`, `providerStatus`, `currentPeriodStart`, `currentPeriodEnd`, `rawLastPayload`.
- `SubscriptionEvent` table: immutable event ledger with dedupe key (`provider + eventId`), signature-verification metadata, processed timestamp, payload hash.
- Keep `Subscription` as your entitlement/read model (what access user has), projected from source/events.

2. Use provider adapters + one orchestrator:
- `ProviderAdapter.verifyPurchase()`
- `ProviderAdapter.parseWebhookEvent()`
- `ProviderAdapter.cancel()`
- `SubscriptionOrchestrator.applyEvent(normalizedEvent)` updates source + entitlement in one transaction.

3. Webhook handling pattern:
- Separate endpoints per provider (`/api/billing/webhooks/apple`, `/api/billing/webhooks/google`, existing Telegram webhook path stays).
- Verify signature/token first, then enqueue to async processor (QStash is fine), then process idempotently from `SubscriptionEvent`.
- Never mutate entitlement directly in webhook controller; only through orchestrator.

4. Receipt validation:
- Mobile purchase flow sends receipt/token to backend.
- Backend validates with Apple/Google, maps product to internal `planId`, writes `SubscriptionSource` + `SubscriptionEvent`, then projects entitlement.
- Reject if user identity is ambiguous (unlinked Apple/Google account vs app user).

5. Subscription state model:
- Separate `accessStatus` from `renewalStatus`.
- `accessStatus`: `ACTIVE|GRACE|EXPIRED`
- `renewalStatus`: `AUTO_RENEW_ON|AUTO_RENEW_OFF|BILLING_RETRY|CANCELED_BY_USER`
- Compute access from period end + grace rules; don’t overload one enum for both concerns.

6. Guard-at-source rules:
- Cancellation endpoint routes by `provider`; Telegram-only API calls must be provider-gated.
- Idempotency key becomes `provider + externalTransactionOrEventId` (not Telegram-specific).
- Plan mapping is explicit (`providerProductId -> internalPlanId`) in DB, not hardcoded.

**Critical Schema Changes**
1. `SubscriptionPayment.telegramChargeId` cannot stay required/unique-only for multi-provider.
2. Add provider and external IDs on payment/source records.
3. Keep the existing unique-active-subscription constraint, but define deterministic precedence if multiple providers overlap (usually “max `currentPeriodEnd` wins” or “latest verified event wins”).

**Rollout Plan (safe)**
1. Introduce new tables + orchestrator behind feature flag; keep Telegram flow writing both old/new models.
2. Migrate Telegram handler to orchestrator (no behavior change).
3. Add Apple/Google purchase validation endpoints and webhook processors.
4. Switch status API to read projected entitlement only; remove Telegram-specific assumptions.
5. Backfill historical Telegram records into `SubscriptionSource/SubscriptionEvent`.

If you want, I can draft the exact Prisma model diff and normalized event contract next (text-only, ready for implementation).

claudeor.content:
Use a provider-agnostic entitlement core, with one adapter per payment provider.

Your current Telegram path already has the right overall shape in [`apps/tma/src/lib/telegram/bot/subscription-payment-handler.ts`](/Users/rsboarder/workspace/bookmatcher/apps/tma/src/lib/telegram/bot/subscription-payment-handler.ts) and [`packages/core/src/lib/backend/monetization/resolveEffectiveStatus.ts`](/Users/rsboarder/workspace/bookmatcher/packages/core/src/lib/backend/monetization/resolveEffectiveStatus.ts): provider-specific ingress, then one canonical subscription/access model. I would extend that pattern, not replace it.

**Recommendation**

- Keep one canonical entitlement model for the app.
- Add provider-specific purchase/transaction records alongside it.
- Put Apple, Google, and Telegram behind the same domain service interface.
- Make webhooks and receipt verification adapters, not business logic.
- Treat provider payloads as evidence, not as your source of truth.

**Canonical model**

- `SubscriptionPlan`: business offering, duration, access tier, price.
- `PlanOffering` or `ProviderOffering`: maps one plan to one provider SKU/product/invoice config.
- `SubscriptionEntitlement`: the user’s actual access state.
- `SubscriptionPurchase` / `EntitlementEvent`: immutable ledger of verified purchase events.
- `ProviderWebhookEvent`: raw inbound event inbox for idempotency, audit, retries.

**Important separation**

- `billingType` is cadence, not channel.
- `provider` is channel, not access.
- `SubscriptionStatus` should describe entitlement state, not mirror every provider’s internal lifecycle.

**Suggested internal state model**

- `ACTIVE`
- `CANCELED`
- `EXPIRED`
- `RENEWAL_FAILED`
- Optional later, if you need it: `GRACE`, `PAUSED`, `PENDING`

Keep provider-specific status in a raw JSON column or provider event record. Don’t let Apple or Google leak their full state machine into the rest of the app unless you truly need it.

**Ingress flow**

```text
Client purchase
  -> provider checkout / SDK
  -> provider callback or webhook
  -> provider adapter validates signature / receipt
  -> raw event stored idempotently
  -> domain command executed
  -> entitlement upserted
  -> cache invalidated + push notification emitted
```

**Webhook handling**

- Telegram Stars:
  - Keep your current `successful_payment` flow.
  - Store `telegram_payment_charge_id` as the provider transaction key.
  - Treat that charge id as the refund handle.
  - If entitlement creation fails after payment, refund through `refundStarPayment`.
- Apple:
  - Use App Store Server Notifications V2.
  - Verify the signed JWS payload on the server.
  - Do not rely on client-side receipt validation as the source of truth.
  - Use App Store Server API as reconciliation/backfill when notifications are delayed or missed.
- Google Play:
  - Use Real-time Developer Notifications as a trigger, not proof.
  - Fetch the authoritative state from `purchases.subscriptionsv2.get`.
  - Verify purchase legitimacy on the backend using the purchase token.
  - Acknowledge the purchase on the backend after granting entitlement.
  - Track `purchaseToken` and `linkedPurchaseToken` for lineage and revocation.

**Receipt validation**

- Apple:
  - `verifyReceipt` is deprecated.
  - Prefer signed transaction / subscription data and server-to-server validation.
- Google:
  - A purchase token is the primary identifier.
  - Backend must verify with Google Play Developer API before granting access.
- Telegram:
  - The successful payment update is your receipt.
  - Preserve the charge id for refund and audit.

**Idempotency**

- Use provider-native immutable ids as unique keys.
- Telegram: `telegram_payment_charge_id`
- Apple: `originalTransactionId` plus transaction id
- Google: `purchaseToken` plus `linkedPurchaseToken` lineage
- Also keep a provider event id if the provider supplies one
- Store the raw event first, then apply the domain transition exactly once

**State management**

- Make one application service own:
  - entitlement upsert
  - purchase ledger write
  - Redis invalidation
  - Pusher emission
  - notification side effects
- All provider adapters call that same service.
- Don’t let each webhook handler mutate Redis or UI caches independently.

That matters because your current system already relies on cache invalidation after a successful subscription mutation. If you split providers without a shared mutation service, you will get stale status reads and inconsistent UI.

**What I would not do**

- Don’t add `if provider === ...` branches across route handlers.
- Don’t make `Subscription` itself store every provider-specific field.
- Don’t use provider status as the app’s access model.
- Don’t treat client callbacks as authoritative without server verification.
- Don’t reuse `billingType` to represent provider identity.

**Practical schema direction**

If I were designing this in your codebase, I’d add:

- `SubscriptionProvider` enum: `TELEGRAM`, `APPLE`, `GOOGLE`
- `SubscriptionPurchase` table with:
  - `provider`
  - `providerTransactionId`
  - `externalSubscriptionId`
  - `purchaseToken` or `originalTransactionId`
  - `userId`
  - `planId`
  - `rawPayload`
  - `verifiedAt`
  - `environment`
  - unique constraint on the provider-native transaction id
- `SubscriptionEntitlement` table or keep the current `Subscription` table as the entitlement aggregate
- `ProviderOffering` table mapping internal plan -> store SKU / bot plan / invoice config

If you want to preserve your existing `Subscription` table, that is fine. Then I’d narrow its job to entitlement state and move provider evidence into a separate purchase ledger.

**Migration path**

- Phase 1: introduce provider-agnostic service interfaces and a shared purchase ledger.
- Phase 2: route Telegram Stars through the shared service.
- Phase 3: add Apple and Google adapters.
- Phase 4: add reconciliation jobs for Apple/Google.
- Phase 5: move reporting/UI to read only from canonical entitlement state.

**Policy caveat**

If this is still the Telegram Mini App itself, Telegram’s Stars docs say digital goods and services inside Telegram apps must use Stars. Apple/Google IAP would apply only if you also ship a separate native mobile app surface. I’m inferring that distinction from your “mobile app” wording, so confirm the distribution surface before implementing.

**Sources**

- [Apple: Validating receipts with the App Store](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/storekit/validating-receipts-with-the-app-store)
- [Apple: Receiving App Store Server Notifications](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/appstoreservernotifications/receiving-app-store-server-notifications)
- [Google Play subscriptionsv2 API](https://developers.google.com/android-publisher/api-ref/rest/v3/purchases.subscriptionsv2)
- [Google Play Billing security guidance](https://developer.android.com/google/play/billing/security)
- [Telegram Stars payments](https://core.telegram.org/bots/payments-stars)

If you want, I can turn this into:
1. a concrete Prisma schema proposal,
2. a route-by-route webhook/API design, or
3. a phased migration plan from the current Telegram-only flow.
